senses.definition: A single asexual cell or stage in some protozoa or algae that divides repeatedly to form many new cells.
examples: The parasite multiplies rapidly during the agamont stage.
Each agamont produces numerous daughter cells inside the host.
Researchers observed the agamont under the microscope.

senses.definition: Plural of agamont: asexual cells or stages in certain protozoa or algae that divide to form many new cells.
examples: Several agamonts were visible in each blood smear.
The tissue sample contained numerous developing agamonts.
Scientists compared the size of agamonts in different species.

senses.definition: Plural of agamospermy: kinds or cases of producing seeds without fertilization in plants.
examples: Different agamospermies have been described in this grass genus.
The book reviews various agamospermies found in crop plants.
These agamospermies complicate the study of plant genetics.

senses.definition: The production of embryos and seeds in plants without fertilization, so the offspring are genetically like the parent.
examples: Agamospermy allows the plant to reproduce without pollination.
In agamospermy, seeds form asexually from ovule tissue.
Agamospermy can help fix desirable traits in crop species.

senses.definition: An asexual spore that can develop into a new plant or organism without fertilization.
examples: Each agamospore can grow into a new individual plant.
The fungus produces an agamospore instead of a sexual spore.
Agamospore formation was observed under laboratory conditions.

senses.definition: Having no visible sex organs or not involving sexual reproduction.
examples: The plant has an agamous form of reproduction.
Agamous species can spread quickly in a stable environment.
Researchers compared agamous and sexually reproducing populations.

senses.definition: Not having a marriage partner or pair bond.
examples: Some people choose an agamous lifestyle and do not marry.
The group promotes agamous relationships outside traditional norms.
She described herself as agamous and uninterested in forming a couple.

senses.definition: A social situation or personal choice in which marriage or pair bonds are not recognized or not formed.
examples: Agamy challenges traditional ideas about family and marriage.
The community’s agamy means there are no formal weddings.
Her agamy is based on a belief in complete personal freedom.

senses.definition: Lacking normal nerve cell clusters called ganglia.
examples: The biopsy showed an aganglionic segment of the colon.
Aganglionic tissue caused a blockage in the infant’s intestine.
Hirschsprung disease is marked by aganglionic bowel.

senses.definition: A female given name used rarely in English, based on an ancient Greek name.
examples: Aganice is mentioned in some historical texts.
They named their daughter Aganice after a famous astrologer.
The character Aganice appears only briefly in the novel.

senses.definition: In Greek mythology, a sacred spring on Mount Helicon that was believed to inspire poets.
examples: Poets were said to drink from Aganippe to gain inspiration.
The legend of Aganippe appears in several classical works.
He compared the library to Aganippe, a source of creative power.

senses.definition: A family name or place name occurring in some languages; it is not commonly used in general English.
examples: Dr. Gao’s colleague, Professor Agao, presented the results.
They traced their roots back to a village called Agao.
The Agao family has lived here for generations.

senses.definition: A family of tiny wasps that pollinate fig trees.
examples: Agaonidae are essential for the life cycle of many figs.
The mutualism between fig trees and Agaonidae is well studied.
Scientists described several new species in the family Agaonidae.

senses.definition: Plural of agape: early Christian communal meals held to express love and fellowship.
examples: The believers gathered for agapae in the evenings.
Historical records describe agapae as simple shared meals.
Some churches still organize agapae to build community.

senses.definition: Another plural form of agape: early Christian meals shared as an expression of brotherly love.
examples: The agapai described in the letter show strong community ties.
Agapai were occasions for prayer and mutual support.
Scholars debate how formal the agapai actually were.

senses.definition: A garden plant with long stems and clusters of blue, purple, or white trumpet-shaped flowers.
examples: Agapanthus looks striking along the garden border.
She planted white agapanthus near the front door.
The agapanthus blooms in midsummer.

senses.definition: Plural of agapanthus: several plants with long stems and round clusters of blue, purple, or white flowers.
examples: Agapanthuses line the driveway on both sides.
We divided the agapanthuses to fill more beds.
The blue agapanthuses make a bold statement.

senses.definition: In Christian thought, unselfish love that cares more about others than oneself.
examples: The charity’s work is inspired by agape.
She tried to show agape to everyone she met.
Agape is often described as unconditional love.

senses.definition: In early Christianity, a communal meal shared as a sign of love and fellowship.
examples: The early church often held an agape after worship.
At the agape they prayed and shared food together.
He wrote about the practice of the Christian agape.

senses.definition: Describing love that is unselfish and caring, especially in a Christian sense.
examples: They tried to build an agape community.
Her agape attitude impressed everyone around her.
The speaker emphasized agape relationships over romantic ones.

senses.definition: Showing or relating to unselfish, self-giving love.
examples: He described God’s agapeic love for humanity.
Agapeic ethics focuses on care and self-giving.
She took an agapeic approach to social justice.

senses.definition: In a way that shows selfless, caring love.
examples: She responded agapeically, putting his needs first.
The volunteers acted agapeically during the crisis.
He tried to live agapeically in his daily choices.

senses.definition: The name of a 19th-century religious community in England, often mentioned in discussions of religious history.
examples: Agapemone attracted both followers and critics.
The scandal at Agapemone made headlines at the time.
She wrote her thesis on the rise and fall of Agapemone.

senses.definition: Relating to the Agapemone religious community or its beliefs.
examples: He studied Agapemonian teachings in detail.
The novel includes an Agapemonian-style cult.
Agapemonian doctrines were widely criticized.

senses.definition: A member or supporter of the Agapemone religious community.
examples: Several agapemonists lived together on the estate.
The press portrayed agapemonists as strange and secretive.
He was raised by agapemonist parents.

senses.definition: Another term for a follower of the Agapemone community.
examples: An agapemonite wrote a detailed defense of the group.
The town was suspicious of the agapemonites.
Former agapemonites later spoke to journalists.

senses.definition: Plural of agape: acts or examples of unselfish, caring love.
examples: Their quiet agapes changed the lives of many people.
The book tells stories of everyday agapes.
She thanked them for their agapes during her illness.

senses.definition: Plural of agape: several communal meals held by early Christians.
examples: The letters mention agapes held after worship.
Early agapes were simple meals shared by believers.
Some churches have revived agapes as a tradition.

senses.definition: In early Christianity, women who lived with male church leaders in a close but supposedly nonsexual relationship.
examples: Church councils condemned the practice of keeping agapetae.
Agapetae were seen by some as spiritual partners.
Controversy over agapetae led to strict rules on clergy.

senses.definition: Plural of agapetus: early Christian men and women living together in a close spiritual, but claimed nonsexual, partnership.
examples: Several bishops warned against relationships with agapeti.
The council issued canons on agapeti and celibacy.
Writers of the time debated whether agapeti were appropriate.

senses.definition: Relating to the insect family Agapetidae, a group of caddisflies.
examples: Agapetid larvae live in clean, running water.
The river supports several agapetid species.
Agapetid adults are rarely seen by casual observers.

senses.definition: A family of small caddisflies whose larvae live in streams and rivers.
examples: Agapetidae are indicators of good water quality.
The study focused on the life cycle of Agapetidae.
Several species of Agapetidae inhabit this mountain stream.

senses.definition: A rare or obsolete term, possibly used as a variant name in mineralogy or as a proper name; it is not in common modern use.
examples: The term agaphite appears only in older catalogs.
Some collectors still refer to the specimen as agaphite.
Modern guides no longer list agaphite as a distinct mineral.

senses.definition: A genus of small parrots, commonly called lovebirds, kept as pets.
examples: Agapornis are popular for their bright colors.
She keeps two Agapornis in a large cage.
Agapornis form strong pair bonds with their mates.

senses.definition: A jelly-like substance from seaweed, used to grow microorganisms in the lab and to thicken some foods.
examples: The bacteria were spread on nutrient agar.
Vegetarian desserts often use agar instead of gelatin.
She boiled the agar before pouring it into Petri dishes.

senses.definition: A mushroom with a cap and stem, especially one from a group that includes the common field mushrooms and toadstools.
examples: The forest floor was dotted with red agarics.
Some agarics are edible, while others are poisonous.
They photographed an agaric growing on the hillside.

senses.definition: A large family of gilled mushrooms that includes many common field and forest species.
examples: Agaricaceae contains both edible and poisonous mushrooms.
The genus Agaricus belongs to the family Agaricaceae.
Several Agaricaceae species fruit in autumn.

senses.definition: Relating to or like mushrooms of the Agaricaceae family, especially with a cap and gills.
examples: The fungus has an agaricaceous appearance.
Agaricaceous forms dominate the autumn mushroom flora.
She specializes in agaricaceous fungi.

senses.definition: An order of fungi that includes most gilled mushrooms.
examples: Agaricales is the largest order of mushroom-forming fungi.
Many familiar edible mushrooms belong to Agaricales.
The diversity of Agaricales is still being described.

senses.definition: Relating to or derived from agaric mushrooms.
examples: The study focused on agaricic compounds in the fungus.
Agaricic toxins can cause severe illness.
They tested the agaricic extract for its effects on insects.

senses.definition: Having the shape of a typical mushroom with a cap and stem.
examples: The fungus has an agariciform cap.
Many agariciform fruiting bodies appear after rain.
An agariciform outline is common in this group.

senses.definition: A chemical substance obtained from certain agaric mushrooms, formerly used in medicine.
examples: Agaricin was once used to treat excessive sweating.
The pharmacist explained that agaricin is rarely prescribed now.
Researchers analyzed the structure of agaricin.

senses.definition: A substance or mixture obtained from agaric mushrooms, especially one with medicinal effects.
examples: The doctor prescribed an agaricine-based remedy.
Manufacturers standardized the amount of agaricine in each tablet.
Agaricine has largely been replaced by newer drugs.

senses.definition: Relating to agaricin or similar substances from agaric mushrooms.
examples: They investigated agaricinic acids in the sample.
Agaricinic compounds may have antimicrobial effects.
The agaricinic fraction was isolated first.

senses.definition: Resembling a typical gilled mushroom in form.
examples: Many wood-inhabiting fungi are agaricoid.
The agaricoid cap is broad and fleshy.
Agaricoid forms dominate this forest.

senses.definition: Plural of agaric: several cap-and-stem mushrooms, often with gills underneath.
examples: Bright agarics appeared after the rain.
Some agarics in this field are safe to eat.
They studied the ecology of the local agarics.

senses.definition: A genus of mushrooms that includes the common cultivated mushroom.
examples: Agaricus is widely grown for food.
Many supermarket mushrooms are species of Agaricus.
She is studying the genetics of Agaricus bisporus.

senses.definition: A family of brightly colored moths, many of which fly during the day.
examples: Agaristidae are known for their vivid wing patterns.
Several Agaristidae feed on grape vines.
The guide includes color plates of Agaristidae.

senses.definition: A thorny shrub of the southwestern United States with yellow flowers and red berries.
examples: Agarita provides food for birds and other wildlife.
They planted agarita along the fence for a natural barrier.
The bright red berries of the agarita are edible.

senses.definition: Having properties like agar, especially being jelly-like and able to form a gel.
examples: The medium has an agaroid consistency.
Agaroid substances are useful as stabilizers.
They tested an agaroid gel in the experiment.

senses.definition: A purified part of agar used to make gels for separating DNA and other molecules in the lab.
examples: The DNA samples were run on an agarose gel.
She melted the agarose before casting the gel.
Agarose is widely used in molecular biology.

senses.definition: Plural of agarose: different types or batches of the gel-forming substance used in laboratory work.
examples: Different agaroses vary in strength and clarity.
They compared three agaroses for DNA separation.
Low-melt agaroses are useful for cloning experiments.

senses.definition: Plural of agar: different kinds or plates of the jelly-like substance used for growing microbes or thickening food.
examples: Several selective agars were used in the experiment.
They poured the agars into Petri dishes.
Different agars support different types of bacteria.

senses.definition: A genus of large brown seaweeds found in cold northern seas.
examples: Agarum grows in dense underwater forests.
Divers collected samples of Agarum for study.
The kelp Agarum provides shelter for many fish.
